mainmatter.p: Define the component \beta _{A}: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( J , A \right ) \mathclose {}} to be the composite s; \alpha _{A}; for the wild naturality structure, we fix f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} to define \beta _{f} : \beta _{A};f = 1 _{ J } ; \beta _{B} as follows:

Next we identify \beta _{J} with 1 _{ J }. By incoherent naturality, we have \alpha _{J};s = \alpha _{I}; thus by our splitting of \alpha _{I} we have \alpha _{J};s = r;s and hence by postcomposing r and canceling via \eta :s;r= 1 _{ I } we conclude that \alpha _J = r. Therefore \beta _J=s; \alpha _J=s;r= 1 _{ J }.

frontmatter.title: A wild universal cone from a splitting of the generic element

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} be a wild category, and let I: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} } be an object of \mathcal {C} such that { \Delta I } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ 1 _{ \mathcal {C} } } is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ \Delta I }}{ \mathcal {C} } to the identity 0-functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ 1 _{ \mathcal {C} } }}{ \mathcal {C} } on \mathcal {C} for some I: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }. Now let r: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( I , J \right ) \mathclose {}} and s: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( J , I \right ) \mathclose {}} be a section-retraction pair witnessed by \eta : s;r = 1 _{ I }, and let \sigma : r;s= \alpha _{I} be a splitting of \alpha _{I}: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( I , I \right ) \mathclose {}}. Then we may define a wild natural transformation { \Delta J } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ 1 _{ \mathcal {C} } } and an identification \chi : \beta _{J} = 1 _{ J }.
(This construction is due to Mike Shulman.)
